start.text:
Leaving a home is a procedure, and the household executes it like one, which is how you both keep it from being anything else.

The beacon comes down first — her beacon, the sixty-one-second lighthouse that found you — coiled and crated and cached behind a false panel with the battery bank, because you are not abandoning Verstand Street, you are, in her phrase, 'suspending operations at the branch office.' The wall of red thread is photographed, then taken down pin by pin and burned, thread by thread, in the tea stove. The maps travel. The originals of everything travel. What cannot travel is cached in three separate places, indexed in two skulls, and the station door is welded behind you by a man from the repair quarter who owes you for the battery trick and asks no questions, because in the Glasshouse's economy, incuriosity is a form of payment.

West, the Continuum's road-letter says, is two hundred kilometres of quilted country to the control lands: the old Ascent Project's groundside campus, decommissioned before the Halfway to a skeleton crew, where the interface archives are — and where, Voss wrote, in the hand of a woman rationing her own hope, 'there are people who can read a key to its keyhole, if the road lets you keep it.'

The road has rules, and the piano man sold you all three of them for a tuning fork: Travel behind the storms, never ahead of them. Pay the Archivists' waystations in memories and the water is free. And at any line of white stones across a road — turn around. That one he would not explain, except to say, softly, re-wrapping the fork like a relic: 'Reclaimers. They're not bandits, friend. Bandits can be priced. These are people with a theory.'

B takes point out of the west portal with his panniers balanced and his ears at cruising trim, and Osy pauses once on the threshold — one look back down the platform at the strung lights of the year she survived alone — and then does not look back again, because the file is closed, and she closes files properly, and the road takes the three of you the way roads have always taken households: one ordinary step at a time, into weather nobody owns.

choices.text: Set the pace by the dog and the clocks — steady, instrumented, behind the storms, the survey discipline scaled up to a pilgrimage.

[Grit 3] Push the first three days hard — long marches, cold camps, distance banked while your bodies are fresh and the country near the city is still mapped. Buy margin now to spend on trouble later.

road_rhythm.text:
The road west teaches you its grammar in the first three days, and the grammar is the survey's, conjugated for distance.

You move behind the storms, as instructed, and the instruction turns out to be a physics lesson: day-storms leave the country behind them settled, the seams freshly fused, the drift gradients slack — a wake of calm you can ride for a day and a half if you read the sky's forgetting right. Your clocks and Pin's storm taxonomy make you, between them, decent sailors of it. B makes you good ones. He has taken to riding the seams' edges like a gull, drifting left or right across the road's width hours before your instruments agree there is anything to drift around, and you have given up logging it as anomaly and started logging it as navigation.

The country itself is the city's lesson at county scale. A silo district running its harvest morning on loop, golden and shining and unenterable. A river that is four seasons wide: ice at the near bank, spring flood midstream, autumn shallows at the far side, crossed on a causeway built of sunk buses by people who charge a toll in stories and let B cross free on grounds of policy. Villages holding their one stuck market-day with lanterns out, trading real bread across the seam to their own shining neighbors out of a courtesy that has stopped being grief and become liturgy.

Evenings, the household runs its liturgy too: camp, clocks, the file — and the board. The travel set is drawn on canvas with shirt-button pieces, and board four continues by firelight, and she is still weather, but you have begun, for the first time in eleven years, to make weather of your own in the middlegame, and three nights ago she looked up from a position you had wrecked on purpose to save a worse one and said, 'Who taught you that?' and you said, 'You did, the long way,' and she looked at the board for a while with an expression that was not about chess.

One entry stays out of the evening talk. On the second night, watching the causeway people work their tollbooth of sunk buses, she says that a toll in stories is the only tariff left that appreciates — dry, exact, pleased with the line — and on the third night, at a different fire, she says it again. Word for word. The same pause before 'appreciates,' the same half-gesture with a captured pawn, and nowhere in her any flicker of a woman repeating herself. You laugh in the right place both times. Then you sit up after she sleeps and log it in the hand you keep for findings you do not want: 'Recurrence, n=1. Subject unaware. Fatigue, probably.' Probably is doing a quantity of work in that sentence that you decline, professionally, to measure — and B, who has arranged himself against her back again, watches you write it with his chin on his paws, and does not close his eyes until you put out the lamp.

On the fourth evening, the road's calm ends at a hill line with a view, and the view is your first waystation: a grain church on a ridge, roof whole, windows candled, and around its yard — pale in the dusk, laid with the care of scripture — a boundary of white stones, with one gap left open, facing the road, like a word left unsaid.

choices.text: White stones mean Reclaimers, and the piano man said turn around — but the gap faces the road like an invitation, and the candles are Archivist candles. Approach slow, hands shown, dog at heel.
Camp short of the ridge tonight and glass the place until you understand who holds it — churches with two congregations are how travelers get enlisted in arguments.

hard_march.text:
You spend the fresh days like a man who has finally found a currency he holds in surplus, and the body — the average body, eleven months hardened and deliberately trained since — pays out.

Three days of long marches: up before the quilted dawns, cold camps, boots dried by fire-stones because flame draws eyes, forty kilometres banked and then forty more, Osy matching you stride for stride on wiry contempt for her own fatigue, B trotting the whole distance twice over with his scouting loops and filing no complaints. The country near the city is mapped and the mapping holds; you cross it while your luck is untested, and by the third dusk you are a week ahead of any schedule the piano man would have priced, camped high on a hill line with the road's first real landmark below.

Distance banked. But the ledger balances the way ledgers do: on the second night, Osy sleeps eleven hours — Osy, who has run on six for the year you've known her, who calls the seventh hour 'interest paid to no one' — and wakes annoyed at the sun, and says nothing, and you say nothing, and both your nothings go in the file. On the third night she deals herself out of the evening game — 'log the position, we'll resume' — and is asleep before the kettle sounds, one hand in B's ruff, and the dog lies still all night under her hand like a man holding a live wire, and in the morning he will not take his scouting loops. He walks at her hip. All day. Recurrence, your log notes, n of one, and the hand that writes it is not as steady as the script.

Below the hill line, in the last light, the road's first waystation shows itself: a grain church on a ridge, roof whole, windows candled — and around its yard, pale and deliberate as scripture, a boundary of white stones, with a single gap left open toward the road, like a word being withheld.

choices.text: Approach slow, hands shown, dog at heel — candles are Archivist candles, whatever the stones say, and your household could use a roof and a still hour.
Glass the place first from the hill. Churches with two congregations are how travelers get enlisted in arguments, and your colleague needs rest more than either faith needs converts.

glass_the_ridge.text:
You glass the grain church through the last of the light, and the reading pays for its hour.

Two congregations, exactly as feared — but not at war. In the candled nave, the grey robes of Archivists move in their unhurried curation. In the yard, by the white stones, camp three figures in the road-leather and slate-grey wool you will come to know very well, each wearing at the throat the small ceramic gorget that is the Reclaimers' one adornment — white, like the stones, like a piece taken off the board. They have their own fire. They do not enter the church. The Archivists bring them bread across the boundary, and the Reclaimers accept it with bowed heads, and the two faiths conduct their coexistence with the scrupulousness of divorced parents at a christening.

'They're not besieging it,' Osy murmurs, glasses up. 'They're auditing it. Look at the tall one.' The tall Reclaimer walks the white boundary at intervals, slow, head tilted, one bare hand held a little away from her body like a woman trailing her fingers in water — and where she pauses, another of them sets a fresh stone. 'They're measuring something. Marking a contour of it. The stones aren't a wall, colleague, they're an isoline.' The glasses come down and her eyes are bright with the day's first real heat. 'An isoline of what, is the question, and I can think of one substance lately of interest, and so can you, and it pools.'

You make cold camp below the hill and watch the candles and the small grey fire late into the dark. B lies against Osy's back, uncommanded — he has done it since the road began, you realize; the file has the entries but you had not yet read them as one series — and toward midnight the tall Reclaimer stops her circuit on the boundary's far side and stands for a long time, face turned out into the dark, toward the country, toward, as near as you can plumb the line of her attention across half a kilometre of night —

Toward your cold, fireless, invisible camp.

choices.text: Morning, then, openly: through the gap in the stones, hands shown, household presented. Whatever she measured in the dark, skulking would confirm the worst reading of it.
Break camp before first light and make the approach with the dog visible and the packs open — nothing declares honest business like luggage a stranger is welcome to inspect.

waystation.text:
The Archivists receive you the way they receive everything: gently, thoroughly, and into an index.

The grain church is a chapel of kept days. Where pews were, trestles; on the trestles, the parish's whole memory — photographs under glass weights, christening gowns in labeled tissue, a shelf of voice recorders each wearing a knitted cosy against the cold — and about it all the grey robes move with their terrible tenderness, reading, always reading, because their scripture is whatever survived. The waystation-keeper, a round soft-voiced man called Brother Alder, feeds you barley soup and the dog a heel of real bread, and takes your waystation-price without ceremony: one memory each, told truly, into the parish recorder. Osy gives an hour of the Glasshouse — the fish woman, the jar, the arithmetic. You give the roof, the rooks, the morning the receiver locked. Neither of you gives the vault, the yard, or the bridge, and Brother Alder, who has taken ten thousand payments and heard what people leave out, holds the pause after your telling exactly one beat too long, and lets it go with the mercy of a professional.

Of the Reclaimers in the yard he says, spreading honey he will not eat himself on bread he presses on you: 'The Wardens? They come when a place begins to hold still. They walk their line and set their stones and mostly they go away again.' He says it lightly. His hands say something else, tidying crumbs that do not exist. 'They believe the stillness is a sickness, you understand. That the world wants to finish breaking — that broken is what true looks like now — and everything that holds a piece of the old day steady is a splinter working against the wound. They pull splinters. It's honest work, mostly, hereabouts. Empty anchors — a stuck barn, a looped lane. They've never yet pulled one that could talk.' He looks up then, mild as milk. 'Though they check, of course. Whenever anyone new comes through. You'll want to be walking their line on your way out. It's only polite, and it's only once, and it doesn't hurt.'

Across the nave, through the candled window, the tall Warden stands at the gap in the white stones, in road-grey, bare-handed, patient as the boundary itself, watching the church door with the specific calm of a woman whose appointment always arrives eventually.

choices.text: Walk the line. Refusing the local custom marks you deeper than any reading could — and you need to know what her bare hand measures, because your household's whole file may hang on it.

[Ledger 4] Trade first, walk second: offer the Wardens something before they ask anything — road intelligence, storm timings, Pin's taxonomy shared over their fire — and meet the reader as a guest instead of a specimen.

warden_trade.text:
You go out to their fire first, as givers, because the oldest move in the Ledger is to change what kind of scene it is before it starts.

Storm timings, the wake-riding trick, two of Pin's cross-sections redrawn from memory on the back of a ration wrapper — road-coin, honest and unpriced, laid at their fire with the manner of travelers paying respect to the local watch. The two junior Wardens receive it with wary courtesy. The tall one receives it with amusement, which is worse, because it is the amusement of a woman watching a good gambit played against a position she has already left.

'You give like a man arranging the light before a portrait,' she says. Her voice is low and unhurried, road-worn at the edges. 'It's well done. Sit. Drink. I'll take the storm timings and thank you — and then you'll walk my line anyway, friend, because the line isn't a toll. It's a diagnosis.' She turns her bare hand over in the firelight, palm up, an instrument presenting itself. 'I was a piano tuner's daughter and a power-grid dowser's granddaughter, and what I am now has no schooling, so hold your methodology questions; I feel the held places the way you'd feel a nail under floorboard. Empty stillness — a stuck barn, a looped lane — feels like a cold coin. But there's another kind. Rare. Warm. A stillness that walks around and doesn't know itself.' She looks at you across the fire, and her eyes in the light are the eyes of the boundary: patient, pale, and set in their theory. 'The doctrine names them moorings-that-breathe, and the doctrine says the world can't finish its honest breaking while they walk it, and I'll tell you the truth I'm not supposed to, since you paid so courteously: I've walked eleven years of lines, and I've felt cold coins by the thousand — and twice, only twice, the warm kind.' A pause, precisely weighed. 'Once was last night. Around midnight. Out on the dark side of the hill.'

choices.text: Walk the line now, in front of everyone, and let her read what she reads — the household doctrine is two eyes open, and it does not exempt you.
Ask her first what the warm kind means in doctrine — pay for the answer if it costs — and then walk the line in front of everyone, exempt from nothing.

warden_line.text:
The walking of the line is a small liturgy, and the yard assembles for it with the unhurried inevitability of country ceremony: the two junior Wardens at their fire, Brother Alder in the church door with his hands folded in his sleeves, the tall Warden waiting at the gap in the stones.

'Left to right, one at a time, slow,' she says. 'It's only once, and it doesn't hurt.' Which is, you note, exactly what Brother Alder said, in the tone of men and women repeating a formula whose exceptions they have decided not to discuss.

Osy goes first — of course she goes first; she has been arranging to go first since the church door, one small unremarkable repositioning at a time, and the sight of it does something to your chest that you file under later. She walks the boundary's length with her tournament walk, and the Warden's bare hand trails the air behind her at half a metre, and at the end the Warden says, 'Thank you,' colorlessly, and then, less colorlessly, with a small frown as if at a note very slightly flat: 'You're drifting, madam. Early yet. You'll know it in the mornings first.' And Osy, who has said nothing to you about the mornings, says, 'Noted,' in her flattest voice, and does not look at you, and the not-looking lands in the file like a dropped blade.

Then B, who trots the line unbidden, tail high, and the Warden laughs — a real laugh, startled out of her — and says, 'A dog below the weave. Cleanest thing I'll touch this year,' and to B directly, with country courtesy: 'Nothing holds you, sir. Go on.'

Then you.

You walk the white stones left to right, slow, in the candled dusk, and you watch her face do what faces do when a formula meets its exception: the professional stillness, arriving one instant too late, over a flare of something that her eleven years of lines have clearly never taught her a container for — and her bare hand, trailing the air behind your shoulder, closes, once, involuntarily, the fist of a woman catching a coin tossed too hard.

A warm one.

choices.text: Stop at the boundary's end and face her. The reading is taken either way; the only question left is whether it gets discussed as colleagues or hunted as doctrine. Choose colleagues, in front of the church.
Hold her gaze while the fist opens — no flinch, no performance of innocence — and let the reading stand as taken. Then the discussion, as colleagues, in front of the church.

warden_verdict.text:
You stop at the end of the stones and you turn and face her, hands open, in front of the church and the fires and your household, and you say, 'Say it out loud, Warden. My colleague and I run on a treaty, and the treaty says the file gets read with both eyes. Whatever your hand just told you, it goes in.'

The yard is very quiet. The two junior Wardens have risen, without drama, the way weather rises. Brother Alder in the doorway has gone the grey of his own robes. And the tall Warden studies you for a long moment across the last white stone — the patient, pale, theoried eyes — and then does the thing you will spend the rest of the road being grateful for and never quite understanding: she steps back, once, off her own line, and shows you both her empty hands.

'Warm,' she says, simply. 'Walking, breathing, mannered, and warm. Eleven years, twice, and the first one I reported.' Something crosses her face like wind over standing grain. 'They took him apart looking for the mechanism. There was no mechanism. There was a shepherd who didn't drift, and then there wasn't, and the world went on exactly as broken as before, and my doctrine calls that finding null and my grandmother's ghost calls it what she'd have called it, which I won't say in a churchyard.' She looks at Osy, then at the dog, then back at you, and when she speaks again it is quiet enough that only the boundary hears it. 'The order will want you the moment I file. So I'll be filing slowly. Three days' slow, westward-blind, badly organized — I have a reputation for thoroughness to spend and this is what it's for. After that, the lines ahead of you get walked by people who report faster than they think.' She sets, with her boot, one white stone out of true — a gate where the gap was, or a signature, or an apology — and says, in the formula's cadence, like a blessing turned inside out: 'It's only once. And it doesn't hurt. Go west, household. Go far, and go now.'

item.name: Three Days' Slow
item.description: A Reclaimer Warden felt what you are — warm, walking, breathing — and chose to file it slowly, westward-blind, spending her reputation for thoroughness like coin. One white stone set out of true at the gap. After three days, the lines ahead get walked by people who report faster than they think.

choices.text: West, that night, by the wake of the next storm — three days of grace on the clock and a household file grown one entry heavier and one secret lighter.
Thank her at the gate-stone — for the grace, for the three days, for the white stone set out of true — and go west that night by the wake of the next storm.

lost_morning.text:
It happens on the second day of the grace period, at the camp under the viaduct, and it happens the way she would have designed it not to: with a witness, in daylight, mid-sentence.

You are breaking camp. Osy is at the map, plotting the day against the storm-wake, talking you through the route in the shorthand the household has grown — and then she stops, between one word and the next, and looks at the map, and looks at the sky, and looks at her own hands, and you watch eleven years of the fastest mind you know arrive at a conclusion and refuse it and arrive at it again.

'Colleague,' she says, in the flattest voice in her register. 'Confirm the day.'

You confirm the day.

'Confirm it again.' You do. She sets down the pencil, with great care, in the exact center of the map, and says: 'I have no yesterday afternoon. I have the viaduct at noon — your bad joke about the toll bridge — and then I have this morning's kettle. Between those —' her hands, which do not shake, are very still in the manner of things being held from shaking — 'there is nothing. Not fog. Not gaps. A clean splice. Somebody has taken six hours of my life out and sewn the ends together, and colleague, the seam is good. If you weren't standing there holding a yesterday I don't have, I would never have found it.'

The Warden's frown by the white stones. You're drifting, madam. You'll know it in the mornings first. And into the silence where your answer should be, B crosses the camp — deliberate, unhurried, a doctor making rounds — and leans his whole weight against her legs, and looks up at her, and Osy looks down at the dog for a long moment, and then says the thing that takes the morning apart:

'He's been doing this since the road began. Leaning. Sleeping against my back. Walking at my hip. I logged it as affection.' Her eyes come up to yours, and they are dry, and they are her sister's cold to the floor of them. 'It isn't affection, is it. He can feel the seams. He's been lying against one every night for a week. Colleague — the dog has known I was losing time since before I lost it, and he has been holding my page in the book with his own body, and I am going to need a minute before I can say anything useful about that.'

She takes the minute. She stands in the camp under the viaduct with her hand in the dog's ruff and her face turned east, toward everything already spent on her, and takes, in eleven years of your acquaintance, the first minute you have ever seen her take.

choices.text: [Cortex 4] Run the assessment properly: memory battery, drift markers, a protocol built from every neuroscience page you ever read — turn the terror into a baseline she can be measured against, because measured is the only mercy she trusts.

Give her the minute, and then give her the file: every observation of B's behavior, dated, from your log — her page held in three hands now, not two. Then plan around the new fact like the household it is.

assessment.text:
You give her the only gift in your inventory equal to the morning: you turn her into a study.

The protocol assembles itself out of every night you ever spent reading neuroscience instead of sleeping — recall batteries, time-estimation trials, the interference tests, a drift diary with fixed anchor events, all of it adapted on the fly for a woman with no yesterday afternoon and a pathological respect for method. You baseline her over two hours under the viaduct: what holds (everything old; the chess is diamond; the sister is diamond; eleven years of you, she says, looking at the results, 'apparently load-bearing masonry'), what frays (the recent, the unrehearsed, the hours no one else witnessed), and the shape of the splice itself, which you map question by question to its edges and find clean beyond any biology you ever read about. Nothing torn. Nothing healing. Edited.

'Say the finding,' she orders, when the battery is done, wrapped in a blanket with tea she has not touched, because being a subject is cold work.

'The loss isn't decay,' you say. 'Decay smears. This excises. Your retention of witnessed time — anything I hold too, anything he guards —' B, against her side, does not stir — 'is intact. What goes is the unwitnessed hours. As if —' and the sentence arrives somewhere your voice does not want to follow, and you make it follow, treaty terms — 'as if whatever keeps accounts has decided that time nobody else can vouch for is time it can take. You're not drifting like the shepherd, or the barn. You're being — collected. Gently. From the edges in. And the dog and I are the two things slowing it down, because we hold copies, and it won't take what's witnessed.'

The viaduct drips. Osy turns her cup a quarter-turn, her tell, the move before the move, and when she speaks it is with the deliberate lightness she reserves for the largest weights: 'Then the protocol writes itself, colleague. Nothing unwitnessed. From here west, we are in each other's sight or in the dog's, all hours, no exceptions — and everything gets said out loud and written twice.' A beat. The cold, and under it, at the very bottom, something that is not cold at all: 'Congratulations. The treaty just became a medical device.'

item.name: Her Baseline
item.description: Two hours of protocol under a viaduct: recall batteries, drift markers, the splice mapped to its clean edges. What's witnessed holds. What's unwitnessed is being collected, gently, from the edges in. The treaty is now a medical device: nothing unwitnessed, all hours, no exceptions.

choices.text: West, under the new protocol — three heartbeats keeping one shared ledger of hours, into the control country.
Log the new protocol in both notebooks before anyone moves — a medical device deserves documentation — then west, three heartbeats, one shared ledger of hours.

file_response.text:
You give her the minute entire, and then you give her the only thing you have that is better than comfort: the record.

Your log, opened flat on the map table, turned to face her — every entry on the dog since the road began, dated, timed, uninterpreted, the way she taught you to keep it. B leans against her back at Millfield camp, 22:40–06:15, unbidden. B declines scouting loops, walks at her hip, day entire. B lies against her spine, hand in ruff, 23:00–dawn; does not shift when the fire spits. A week of it, in your impatient hand, meaning nothing at the time and meaning, read as one series in the viaduct's grey light, exactly one thing: her page, held, night after night, by thirty-four kilos of quiet insistence that the book stay open.

Osy reads the series twice. Then she closes the log, and squares it to the table's edge, and sits for a moment with her hand flat on its cover, the way you square things when your hands need governing.

'Amendments to the treaty,' she says at last, in the voice she builds bridges out of. 'One: nothing unwitnessed. From here west we stay in each other's sight or in his, all hours — if the ledger only takes what nobody can vouch for, then we vouch. For all of it. Two: the log doubles. You keep me and I keep you, hour against hour, because if it can splice my afternoons it can splice your — anything, and we will not find out late.' Her hand comes off the log cover. 'Three.' She looks down at B, who looks back up at her with the face that has no theory, only the practice, and she says — to him, formally, in front of you, as the household's second witness — 'Your instincts outrank my dignity, sir. Retroactive to the beginning of the road. Lean where you must.'

B, hearing the tone if not the treaty, thumps his tail once on the cold stone, ratifying, and leans.

choices.text: West, under the amended treaty — three heartbeats keeping one shared ledger of hours, into the control country.
Rest your hand once on the dog's head — retroactive gratitude, ratified where he can feel it — then west, under the amended treaty, into the control country.

end_ch7.text:
The road west, after the viaduct, is a different road, because the household on it is a different instrument.

You travel now as one organism with three kinds of attention: her pattern-hunger, your instrumented patience, and the dog's subscription to whatever frequency the world's seams sing on. The protocol holds — nothing unwitnessed, hours logged double, the evening board resumed with the shirt-button pieces even on the bad nights, especially on the bad nights, because board four is also now a memory battery, and every finished game is six hundred moves of vouched-for time. She loses no more mornings that week. Whether that is the protocol, or the dog, or the ledger west of the viaduct simply having nothing yet to spend, the file declines to say. Files are like that, she reminds you. They never volunteer.

The country climbs and empties. Quilted farmland gives way to moor, moor to the long glacial valleys of the control lands' approach, and the seams out here grow strange: older, slower, whole hillsides holding decades rather than days, so that you walk an afternoon along the shore of a lake that is also, faintly, shiningly, a village drowned forty years before the dam. B rides the edges. The clocks agree more than they should. Something out west, you both note in the same evening's entry, is combing the country's time the way the yard combed the wind.

On the last night before the Continuum's marked turnoff, camped in the lee of a fallen pylon with the board between you, Osy sets down a shirt-button rook mid-move and says, conversationally, into the firelight: 'Inventory. A yard that spends a city on keeping me. An Editor in your hand. A Warden who felt you warm. A ledger collecting my unwitnessed hours. You realize what the file is converging on, colleague — the shape under all of it.' She does not wait; the household does not flinch at pages. 'Whatever broke the world left exactly two anomalies walking around loose in it, and they found each other in the dark inside of a month, and one of them is being spent on and the other is being — preserved. We're not investigators, colleague. We never were.' She picks the rook back up, and plays it, and looks at you across the only lit square of the whole dark moor. 'We're evidence. Somebody's evidence. And tomorrow we walk into the one place left that might still be able to read us. Your move.'
